title: Riverton Launches Office-to-Housing Pilot to Unlock 215 Affordable Units
key_facts: Pilot funds: $22 million from housing fund and federal ARPA remainder
Three buildings selected for first phase: 114 Cedar Tower (85 units), Northline Annex (72 units), Harbor Exchange (58 units)
Target affordability: 60% of units at ≤50% Area Median Income (AMI), 30% at 60–80% AMI, 10% supportive housing
Office vacancy rate downtown: 22% as of Q3 2026
Permitting target: 120-day review for eligible conversions

quotes.quote: We are turning empty floors into front doors for working families.
quotes.speaker: Elena Park, mayor, City of Riverton

quotes.quote: This pilot is designed to move quickly while keeping homes affordable for decades.
quotes.speaker: Marco Silva, housing director, Riverton Department of Housing and Community Development

details: The pilot aims to accelerate office-to-residential conversions through targeted grants, fast-track permitting, and tax incentives. Affordability covenants will last 35 years.

title: Ordinance 24-198: Adaptive Reuse and Affordability Incentives
key_facts: Ten-year declining tax abatement for qualifying conversions
Parking minimums eliminated within 0.5 miles of rapid transit
Inclusionary requirement: minimum 80% of units affordable up to 80% AMI
Prevailing wage and apprenticeship utilization required for projects receiving city funds
Life-safety code compliance required; flexibility allowed for non-structural elements

quotes.quote: The ordinance balances speed, safety, and long-term affordability.
quotes.speaker: Council President Rosa Jimenez, Riverton City Council

details: The ordinance creates a pathway for adaptive reuse with defined affordability targets and labor standards. It clarifies code interpretations for conversion layouts while maintaining seismic and fire safety requirements.

title: Planning Department Briefing: Feasibility of Office-to-Residential Conversions
key_facts: Estimated average conversion cost: $210,000 per unit vs. $300,000 for new construction
Greenhouse gas savings of up to 35% per unit relative to new builds
Potential long-term yield: 1,100 units if 12–15 buildings convert over five years
Primary constraints: deep floor plates (>60 feet), window access, elevator cores, egress requirements
First occupancy projected: Q4 2027 for 114 Cedar Tower

quotes.quote: Conversions are not a silver bullet, but they are a faster path for suitable buildings.
quotes.speaker: Aisha Romero, chief planner, Riverton Department of City Planning

details: Methodology draws on building surveys, pro forma modeling, and case studies from comparable cities. The memo proposes a 120-day permit service standard for qualifying projects.

title: City Council Hearing on Ordinance 24-198: Public Comment Excerpts
key_facts: Thirty-two speakers testified; 21 in support, 8 with concerns, 3 opposed
Small business owners emphasized foot traffic recovery; tenant advocates urged deeper affordability

quotes.quote: Empty towers don’t buy lunch. Residents do.
quotes.speaker: Raj Patel, owner, Cedar Street Deli

quotes.quote: Without more units at 30% AMI, many of our neighbors will still be locked out.
quotes.speaker: Danielle Ortega, organizer, Riverton Tenants Union

quotes.quote: We can deliver homes faster if approvals are predictable and timelines are real.
quotes.speaker: Ava Morales, development director, Harbor Partners

details: The hearing captured diverse perspectives, focusing on affordability levels, construction timelines, and downtown recovery.

title: Memorandum of Understanding: Pilot Partnerships for Adaptive Reuse
key_facts: Partners: Harbor Partners (114 Cedar Tower), Northline Development (Northline Annex)
Affordability period: 35 years; penalty for non-compliance equals 150% of abatement value
ADA accessibility: minimum 5% fully accessible units; additional 10% adaptable
Supportive services provider: Bridges Home for on-site case management at Harbor Exchange

quotes.quote: The MOU locks in affordability and services through binding covenants.
quotes.speaker: Sarah Kim, counsel, City of Riverton Law Department

details: The agreement sets milestones for financing close by Q2 2027 and outlines reporting requirements to the city’s oversight committee.

title: Adaptive Reuse Economics in Mid-Sized Downtowns
publisher: Center for Urban Markets, State University of Cascadia
author: Dr. Steven Wu
key_facts: Typical conversion costs run 20–35% below comparable new construction for suitable buildings
Common risks: daylighting in deep floor plates, HVAC retrofits, and change-of-use code upgrades
Cost overruns of 15–25% are common when floor plates exceed 60 feet

quotes.quote: Adaptive reuse works best in medium-height, post-1970 buildings with regular floor plates.
quotes.speaker: Dr. Steven Wu, housing economist, State University of Cascadia

details: The memo synthesizes project data from 11 cities and outlines conditions for financially viable conversions.

title: Ensuring Conversions Serve the Lowest-Income Residents
author: Jessica Nguyen
key_facts: Recommends at least 20% of units at or below 30% AMI
Calls for on-site services and tenant selection through a public lottery
Warns against over-reliance on shallow affordability bands

quotes.quote: Affordability must reach the people most at risk of displacement.
quotes.speaker: Jessica Nguyen, executive director, Riverton Housing Alliance

details: The letter supports conversions with deeper affordability, tenant protections, and transparent oversight.

title: How Cities Are Reimagining Offices After the Pandemic
key_facts: Several West Coast cities report early conversion projects bringing 1,500+ units online
Market conditions vary; high-rise Class A towers remain difficult to convert

quotes.quote: Conversions are no panacea, but they are one tool among many.
quotes.speaker: Editorial board, The Cascadia Tribune

details: A regional roundup of office-to-housing efforts, highlighting successes and challenges in comparable markets.

title: Life-Safety and Seismic Considerations in Office-to-Residential Conversions
author: Maria Lopez, PE
key_facts: Seismic retrofits can add 8–12% to project costs in older structures
NFPA-compliant egress and sprinkler upgrades are mandatory; corridor pressurization often required

quotes.quote: Safety upgrades are non-negotiable and must be budgeted up front.
quotes.speaker: Maria Lopez, PE, principal, Lopez Code Consulting

details: Brief outlines key building code hurdles, with recommended sequencing to reduce delays during change-of-use reviews.

title: Pilot Evaluation Framework and KPIs
publisher: Mayor’s Office of Innovation, City of Riverton
key_facts: Quarterly public dashboards tracking permits, units, affordability mix, and timelines
Success thresholds: at least 200 units permitted within 18 months; average permit time ≤120 days
Independent oversight committee with tenant, small business, and labor representatives

quotes.quote: We will report progress quarterly so residents can hold us accountable.
quotes.speaker: Leah Ortiz, director, Mayor’s Office of Innovation

details: Evaluation plan defines metrics, data sources, and reporting cadence for the pilot program.
